C6/9.description: Lush John Mayer style IV chord.

Badd11 (High Drone).description: Massive I chord — open E adds the 11.

Co7 (+3 frets).description: Exact same shape, up a minor 3rd

Co7 (+6 frets).description: Exact same shape, up a tritone

Fmaj7#11 (Form 1).description: E shape at 1fr — use instead of plain F for a dreamy Lydian color.

F#7add11 (Form 1).description: Use instead of F#7 — open B is the 11.

G6 (Form 1).description: Use instead of G — open e adds the 6.

Aadd9 (Form 1).description: The A-key workhorse — open B is the 9.

Cmaj7 (Form 1).description: Use instead of C — open B is the maj7.

D6/9 (Form 1).description: Use instead of D — 6 and 9 ring on top.

Cmaj7 (Form 2).description: Asus2 shape at 3fr — use instead of C.

D6sus2 (Form 2).description: Use instead of D — floating sus2 with a 6 on top.

G#m7b13 (Form 3).description: Dark minor color — open E is the b13.

Am9 (Form 3).description: Full Am9 — open B is the 9.

C#m7 (Form 3).description: 5th omitted — the open-string nuance still carries.

D#m7 (Form 3).description: 5th omitted; open strings rub (b9) — passing color, mind the clash.

Em7 (Form 3).description: Top of the form — pure Em7 at the octave.

G#m b13 (Form 4).description: Fuller G#m — open E adds the b13 shimmer.

C#m7 (Form 4).description: The 9fr hi-position C#m7 — full voicing with 5th.

Aadd9/C# (Form 5).description: 1st-inversion A — use for A/C#, open B is the 9.

Bsus4/D# (Form 5).description: Rising bass line — two frets up from A/C#.

Cmaj7/E (Form 5).description: Use for C/E — open B turns it maj7.

D6/9/F# (Form 5).description: Use for D/F# in ascending bass runs.

E/G# (Form 5).description: The E-key resolution point of the climb.

Fmaj7#11/A (Form 6).description: Use for F/A — half-step riser with open-string haze.

F#7add11/A# (Form 6).description: Use for F#/A# — keeps the chromatic bass climbing.

G6/B (Form 6).description: Use for G/B at the top of the climb.

D6/9/F# (Form 6).description: Use for D/F# in descending bass lines.

G6/B (Form 7).description: One finger — B bass under a ringing G6.

C (A-Shape).description: A-shape barre at 3fr — the second CAGED station.

C (G-Shape).description: The hardest CAGED grip — many players fret just the top four strings.

C (E-Shape).description: E-shape barre at 8fr — the workhorse barre chord.

C (D-Shape).description: D-shape at the 10th fret — bright top-string voicing.

F5 (Moveable).description: Slide anywhere — the root names the chord.

E9 (Funk).description: The James Brown chord — snap it on the "and".

E13 (Open).description: Open-string 13th — huge dominant color for cheap.

Esus4 (Open).description: The 4th leaning back into E major.

barreGroups.description: Muddy but necessary for completing the cycle. Strings E-A-D-G.
The absolute workhorse for comping. Strings A-D-G-B.
High register. Cuts through the mix. Strings D-G-B-e.
Thick traditional jazz sound. Root on 6th string.
Play triads over a bass note to form lush extensions.
Must-know chords for resolving to minor keys.
The same C major mapped through all five CAGED shapes up the neck. Learn the map, own the fretboard.
G7 drop-2 inversions on the middle strings — pair with the maj7 and m7 matrices for full ii-V-I coverage.
Bm7b5 drop-2 inversions — the gateway chord of every minor ii-V-i.
A dim7 chord repeats itself every three frets — one grip gives you four chords.
Stacked perfect 4ths (McCoy Tyner) and the Eric Johnson drop-2&4 spread.

electricGroups.description: Eric Johnson / Frusciante style. Spanning across skipped strings.
G-B-e strings. Essential for R&B accompaniment.
The absolute lowest rhythmic foundation. Replaces the bass player.
Gospel and Neo-Soul passing chords — diminished pulls down, augmented pushes up.
Rootless 2-note voicings for Cory Wong style funk comping.
Massive 5ths that span multiple skipped strings.
Dense stacked 2nds for volume swells and heavy reverb pads.
Hendrix / Mayer style: thumb takes the bass so fingers are free to decorate.
Roots and fifths, moveable everywhere. Palm-mute for chug, open up for choruses.
James Brown-approved dominant stabs. Slide in from a half-step below.
The fourth triad string-set — F major inversions living under the wound strings.

tensionForms.description: Start from the open E chord and slide the whole grip up while strings 1-2 keep ringing. Each stop lands on a chord you can use instead of the plain one: F → Fmaj7(#11), F# → F#7(add11), G → G6, A → Aadd9, B → Badd11, C → Cmaj7, D → D6/9.
Asus2 with the bass on the 5th string. Slide up two frets for Bsus4, three for Cmaj7, one more for the moody C#m7, then Dsus2 territory and on up to F#m11 and G6 at the high frets.
The minor-family form: mute the 5th string and move root + b7 + b3 under the open strings. Some stops omit the 5th entirely — the open-string nuance still carries the sound. Watch the b9 rub on D#m7.
The fuller four-finger minor grip, at home around the 9th fret (C#m7) and slid down for Bm and Am, or walked F#m → G#m → Am for horizontal motion across the board.
First-inversion slash voicings for sophisticated bass motion: A(add9)/C# → Bsus4/D# → Cmaj7/E → D6/9/F# → E/G#. The classic E-key ascending bass line, fully harmonized.
A compact slash form with the bass on the 6th string. Climb it in half/whole steps for tension — F/A → F#/A# → G/B — and keep D/F# handy for descending runs.

tensionForms.formKr: 제7폼: G6/B 및 기타 변형 폼
tensionForms.description: The lecturer's signature walk: a one-finger G6/B, then the Form-5 grips carry the bass and tensions up together — G6/B → A/C# → Bsus4/D# → C/E — resolving home on E/G#.

progressions.title: The 1-5-6-4 Anthem (Pop/Rock)
progressions.example: G Major → D/F# → Em7 → C add9

progressions.title: The Borrowed "Minor 4"
progressions.example: C Major → F Maj 7 → F Minor → C Major

progressions.title: ii-V-I Minor Turnaround
progressions.howTo: Focus on the altered notes pulling to the minor root.

progressions.title: Pedal Point Rhythm
progressions.howTo: Hold the G bass note while shifting triads.

progressions.title: The Gospel Walk-Up
progressions.howTo: Keep the progression moving smoothly by anticipating the bass notes.

progressions.title: Neo-Soul Turnaround
progressions.description: Thick, rootless extensions resolving to minor.
progressions.howTo: Use the rootless Cmaj9 to stay out of the way, then create heavy tension with E7#9.

progressions.title: 12-Bar Blues (Key of E)
progressions.description: The most-played progression in history: I7-IV7-V7.
progressions.howTo: Four bars of E7, two of A7, two of E7, then B7-A7-E7-B7 to turn around. Shuffle the 8ths.

progressions.title: 50s Doo-Wop (I–vi–IV–V)
progressions.description: The "Stand By Me" changes — hundreds of songs live here.
progressions.howTo: Two beats per chord with a light swing; keep the index anchored moving C → Am.

progressions.title: Sus Breathing (D)
progressions.description: The 2nd and 4th orbit the 3rd — instant campfire motion from one chord.
progressions.howTo: Keep index and ring planted; lift and add the middle and pinky to swing between sus colors.

licks.title: Chord Tone Targeting
licks.description: Landing on the 3rd of the next chord.
licks.howTo: Play a D minor line, ending exactly on the B note when G hits.

licks.title: Acoustic Blues Turnaround
licks.howTo: Slide the diminished shape up and back down.

licks.title: Country 6ths Glide
licks.howTo: Pluck the G and high-E strings together, sliding between inversions.

licks.title: Funk 9th Slide
licks.description: The classic funk stab — approach the 9th grip from below.
licks.howTo: Strike the grip one fret low and slide up landing on beat one; choke with the fretting hand for the "chank".

techniques.title: Hybrid Picking
techniques.description: The absolute foundation of modern electric accompaniment.
techniques.howTo: Hold the pick with thumb/index. Pluck the G string with the pick, and simultaneously snap the B and e strings with your middle and ring fingers.

techniques.title: Volume Swells
techniques.description: Creates a synth-pad like wash of sound.
techniques.howTo: Wrap your pinky around the volume knob. Strike the cluster with the volume at zero, then slowly roll the pinky up.

techniques.title: Dynamic Palm Muting
techniques.description: Controlling the energy of a building section.
techniques.howTo: Rest the fleshy side of your picking hand lightly against the bridge saddles. Lift off slightly on the downbeats.

techniques.title: Systematic Arpeggiation
techniques.example: Rake Down → Move Shape → Rake Up
techniques.description: Turning static chords into flowing textures.
techniques.howTo: Do not strum. Let the pick fall through each string individually, maintaining a steady 16th-note subdivision.

techniques.title: Neo-Soul Double Stop Slide
techniques.howTo: Barre the double stop one fret below the target, pick once, and instantly slide up into pitch.

techniques.title: Travis Picking
techniques.howTo: Keep the thumb bouncing between the A and D strings while the fingers pick syncopated melody notes.

techniques.title: 16th Note Funk Struts
techniques.howTo: Right hand never stops moving in 16ths. Use the fretting hand to choke the chord for percussive "chucks".

techniques.title: Anchor Fingers
techniques.description: Index and middle never move between C and Am — the fastest chord change you own.
techniques.howTo: Change chords by moving only the ring finger. Anchored fingers keep the transition silent and instant.

techniques.title: Palm-Mute Chugging
techniques.description: The bedrock of rock rhythm guitar.
techniques.howTo: Rest the picking-hand heel on the bridge saddles, downpick straight 8ths, open the mute for accents.

quiz.question: E 키와 A 키에서 오픈 텐션 코드를 사용할 때, 주로 어떤 줄을 개방현으로 유지합니까?
quiz.answer: 주로 1번 줄과 2번 줄을 개방현으로 사용합니다. 이는 코드에 고정된 텐션감을 부여하여 입체적인 사운드를 만듭니다.

quiz.question: 제1폼에서 F 코드 위치를 잡고 1, 2번 줄을 개방하면 어떤 코드가 형성됩니까?
quiz.answer: Fmaj7 (add #11) 코드가 됩니다. 일반적인 F 코드가 나올 자리에 편곡된 버전으로 사용할 수 있습니다.

quiz.question: 복잡한 코드 네임을 모두 외워야 합니까? 그 이유는 무엇입니까?
quiz.answer: 굳이 모두 외울 필요는 없습니다. 원래의 기본 코드(예: F, F#) 대신 사용할 수 있다는 점을 기억하고 활용하는 것이 더 중요합니다.

quiz.question: Asus2 폼(제2폼)에서 두 칸 이동하여 연주하면 어떤 코드가 됩니까?
quiz.answer: Bsus 계열 코드가 됩니다. 동일한 폼을 유지한 채 프렛 위치만 이동하여 연주가 가능합니다.

quiz.question: F#m7 (add 11) 폼을 이동하여 연주할 때, 일부 코드에서 공통적으로 생략되는 음은 무엇입니까?
quiz.answer: 5도음이 주로 생략됩니다. 5도음이 없어도 오픈 텐션 코드 특유의 뉘앙스는 충분히 살아 있습니다.

quiz.question: 분수 코드 A/C# 폼에서 두 칸 이동했을 때 만들어지는 코드의 이름은 무엇입니까?
quiz.answer: Bsus4/D# 코드가 됩니다. 베이스 음이 이동함에 따라 코드의 성격이 변하는 분수 코드의 형태입니다.

quiz.question: 오픈 텐션 코드가 일반적인 코드에 비해 가지는 사운드 측면의 장점은 무엇입니까?
quiz.answer: 훨씬 풍부하고 입체감 있는 사운드를 낼 수 있습니다. 개방현의 지속적인 울림이 코드 진행에 깊이를 더합니다.

quiz.question: 강의자가 추천하는 '음악적 고정관념을 벗어나는 방법'은 무엇입니까?
quiz.answer: 화성학 이론이나 고정관념을 잠시 내려놓고, 지판 위 폼 이동과 소리의 어울림을 자유롭게 시도해 보는 것입니다.

quiz.question: E/G# 코드를 대체하여 사용할 수 있는 하행 진행용 코드는 무엇입니까?
quiz.answer: D/F# (또는 D6/9/F#) 코드입니다. 베이스 러닝을 포함한 하행 코드 진행에서 유용합니다.

quiz.question: 이 7가지 코드 폼은 반드시 E 키나 A 키에서만 사용해야 합니까?
quiz.answer: 아닙니다. 주로 E, A 키에서 쓰이지만 앞뒤 코드와 잘 어울린다면 다른 키에서도 자유롭게 활용할 수 있습니다.

glossary.definition: 지판을 누르지 않은 개방현을 포함하여 연주하는 코드.
기본 3화음·7화음 이외의 음(9, 11, 13도 등)을 더해 긴장감과 색채감을 주는 음.
왼손으로 지판을 누르지 않고 줄 본연의 소리를 내는 상태.
'A/C#'처럼 기본음이 아닌 다른 음을 베이스로 지정한 코드.
지판 위에서 코드를 잡는 손가락의 모양이나 형태.
구성음의 위치를 바꾸어 베이스 음을 변경하는 것 (예: A 코드에서 C# 베이스).
동시에 울리는 음들이 충돌하는 느낌을 주는 화음 — 특정 위치 이동 시 주의.
